specification.topic-statement: The logger writes structured JSON log records to a rotating file in a well-known directory, with thread-safe serialization and automatic log rotation on each initialization.

scope.in-scope: Log directory creation and path resolution, log file rotation on initialization, per-record formatting (level, ISO 8601 timestamp with milliseconds and timezone offset, payload), thread-safe file writes, and the two logger modes (global singleton and dependency-injected instance).
scope.boundary: How log records are read, searched, or visualized is outside this scope. The logger only produces the log file.

field.name: level
field.note: severity label: debug, info, warn, or error

field.name: timestamp
field.note: ISO 8601 format with millisecond precision and UTC offset; e.g. 2026-01-15T14:23:05.123+00:00

field.name: logline
field.note: the original structured payload passed by the caller

behavior.initialization:
Initialization resolves the log file path and opens the file for appending.
If the environment variable IKIGAI_LOG_DIR is set, the log file is placed at that path appended with "/current.log". Otherwise, the log directory is ".ikigai/logs" relative to the supplied working directory, and the log file is "current.log" within that directory. The directory is created if it does not exist.
If the resolved path exceeds the internal path buffer capacity, the process terminates via the panic handler.
If a file named "current.log" already exists at the resolved location, it is renamed to an archive file before the new log is opened. The archive filename is derived from the current local time formatted as an ISO 8601 datetime, with colon characters replaced by dashes to produce a filesystem-safe name. This rotation occurs on every initialization, so each process run begins with a fresh log file.
The log file is opened for writing. Failure to open the file terminates the process via the panic handler.
behavior.write-operation:
Each write composes a JSON object from the level string, a freshly captured timestamp, and the caller-supplied payload, then appends it to the log file as a single line followed by a newline character (JSON Lines format).
The timestamp is captured at write time using the current wall clock. It includes both seconds and milliseconds. The UTC offset is formatted as "+HH:MM" (or "-HH:MM" for negative offsets).
The JSON object is serialized using a JSON library. The caller transfers ownership of the payload document to the logger; the document is freed after serialization whether or not the write succeeds.
The serialized string is written to the file. A mutex serializes all write operations so that concurrent callers from multiple threads do not interleave their output.
behavior.logger-modes:
Two logger modes exist and operate independently.
A single global logger is initialized once at startup via a dedicated initialization function and written to via a global write function. If the global logger was not initialized, write calls are silent no-ops. This mode is used by subsystems that predate the dependency-injected instance logger.
An instance logger is allocated as a talloc context and carries its own file handle and mutex. It is initialized with the same path resolution logic as the global logger. When the talloc context is freed, the file handle is closed and the mutex is destroyed. This mode is intended for components that receive their logger via dependency injection.
If the instance logger's file handle is absent, write calls are silent no-ops.
behavior.error-behavior:
Directory creation failure (other than the directory already existing), path buffer overflow, and file open failure all terminate the process via the panic handler. These conditions are treated as unrecoverable.
Write failures after initialization do not terminate the process.
